events.name: Society of Capital Design Launch
events.description: Students from around the world joined the official launch of SCD, where the society's vision, annual programmes, and opportunities were introduced through keynote talks and an interactive Q&A.

events.name: Speaker Session: Financial Due Diligence and Valuations
events.description: How do investors verify whether a company's numbers are real before they commit capital, and how do they determine what a business is actually worth? Aneeza Haidar, CEO of RMB Group, covers the due diligence process, the three standard valuation methods, and what happens when nobody checks the story. Includes a case study on the Wirecard collapse.

events.name: Speaker Session: Designing for 50°C: Engineering Infrastructure in Extreme Heat
events.description: Concrete cures differently at 50°C. Steel expands. Asphalt softens. Every material behaves differently in extreme heat, and Gulf engineers design for conditions that violate the assumptions in most international building codes. This session covers material selection, thermal expansion calculations, passive cooling in structures, and the trade offs between cost, durability, and performance when the environment works against you.

events.name: The Capital Design Challenge
events.description: SCD's flagship inter-school competition. Teams of three to four, each with at least one economics and one engineering member, receive a real infrastructure case set in the Gulf region and must design a solution, cost it within a capped budget, and deliver a video pitch. After submissions close, all teams join a live session where an unseen budget change is revealed. Thirty minutes to adapt, three minutes to present, one follow up question from judges. The current case is the Al Reem District Problem, with an AED 40 million capital budget.

cycles.name: Cycle 1 Winter 2025
cycles.econ_prompts:
Pick one company that went from startup to billion dollar valuation. Explain the financial decisions that got it there and identify which single decision mattered most.
Choose a product or service you use that appears to be free. Explain how the company behind it makes money and argue whether the user is really getting a good deal.
Choose one financial crisis from the last 30 years. Explain what caused it, how it spread, and what one regulation introduced afterwards was designed to prevent.
cycles.eng_prompts:
Choose one engineering failure (a bridge collapse, a building fault, a product recall). Explain what went wrong technically and what should have been done differently.
Pick one material that changed an industry (carbon fibre, silicon, reinforced concrete, or another). Explain the properties that made it useful and how the industry worked before it existed.
Choose one system that converts energy from one form to another (a power station, a car engine, a solar panel). Explain where energy is lost in the process and propose one realistic improvement.

cycles.name: Cycle 2 Spring 2026
cycles.econ_prompts:
Pick one industry that has been disrupted by fintech (payments, lending, insurance, or another). Explain what the traditional model looked like, what replaced it, and who lost out in the change.
Choose one country whose currency lost significant value in the last decade. Explain what caused the decline and how it affected ordinary people's purchasing power.
Pick one investment that most people consider safe (property, gold, government bonds, or another). Argue whether it is actually as safe as people believe, using evidence.
cycles.eng_prompts:
Choose one city or country facing a serious water shortage. Explain the engineering solutions available and argue which one best balances cost, scale, and environmental impact.
Pick one technology that exists today but is too expensive for widespread use (space travel, hydrogen fuel, lab grown meat, or another). Explain what engineering breakthroughs would need to happen to bring the cost down.
Choose one structure or machine built more than 100 years ago that still functions today. Explain what made it last and what modern engineers can learn from its design.

cycles.name: Cycle 3 Summer 2026
cycles.econ_prompts:
1. Pick one company or bank that collapsed in the last 20 years. Explain what the warning signs were before it failed and argue whether investors should have seen it coming.

2. Choose one market (housing, crypto, stocks, or another) that experienced a bubble. Explain how prices got so far from reality and what finally brought them back down.

3. Pick one country that transitioned from a resource dependent income to a diversified one (or is currently attempting to). Explain the financial strategy behind the shift and assess how well it is working.
cycles.eng_prompts:
1. Choose one form of renewable energy and one fossil fuel source. Compare their full engineering lifecycles from construction to decommissioning and argue which produces more usable energy per unit of total cost.

2. Pick one medical technology (MRI, prosthetics, pacemakers, or another). Explain how it works at a technical level and how its engineering has improved since it was first introduced.

3. Choose one mega project currently under construction anywhere in the world. Explain the biggest engineering challenge it faces and evaluate the solution being used.

results.title: Cycle 1 Winter 2025
results.meta: **6 winning responses across Finance and Engineering tracks**

text.home_tagline: Bridging Finance and Engineering for Students Worldwide
text.home_intro: Founded by students in 2025 and run through school chapters across the UAE. Finance students and engineering students work the same problems here, from budget caps to load limits.

event_archive.title: SCD Launch
event_archive.description: The Society of Capital Design executed its official launch to formally and publicly introduce the organization and its leadership. The session detailed the core mission and explained exactly what the society does at the intersection of finance and engineering. Finally, the team presented a comprehensive overview of how the organization will benefit its student members.

event_archive.title: Speaker Session: Financial Due Diligence and Valuations
event_archive.description: Aneeza Haidar, CEO of RMB Group, delivered a session on how investors verify a company's financials before committing capital and the three standard methods used to value a business. The session covered quality of earnings analysis, red flags in financial statements, and included a case study on the Wirecard collapse. A Q&A followed.
event_archive.speaker: Aneeza Haidar, CEO of RMB Group

event_archive.title: The Capital Design Challenge
event_archive.description: SCD ran its flagship inter-school competition, where mixed economics and engineering teams designed and costed a solution to the Al Reem District heat exposure problem under a capped AED 40 million budget. Teams submitted a two page summary sheet and an eight minute video pitch, then joined a live session where the annual operations budget was cut from AED 2.5 million to AED 1.5 million without warning. Teams had thirty minutes to adapt before delivering a live three minute twist response to judges

event_archive.title: Speaker Session: Designing for 50°C: Engineering Infrastructure in Extreme Heat
event_archive.description: The session covered how civil and structural engineers adapt standard construction methods for extreme heat environments, including material behaviour at high temperatures, passive cooling strategies in building and infrastructure design, and the trade offs between cost and thermal performance. The speaker walked through real examples from Gulf construction projects where international building code assumptions had to be reworked for local conditions.
